air humidity is perfect for the colorful fungus which grows on the walls.
Yellow, red and dark brown patches cover them in many places. The roof of the
tunnel is made from wedge-shaped stones that have been laid cleverly into an
arch. Some of the sharp stones project below the others, and man or beast must
bow humbly to pass them.
name: Secret Way

desc:
A high wooden fence closes this section of grass off from the areas around it.
The only entrance and exit to this pasture exists in the form of a wooden gate
in the north. The ground is covered by short grass that has given way to a patch
of dirt just in front of the gate. A small wooden building stands to the east
with part of its roof reaching into this pasture and providing some shelter.
name: A Small Pasture

desc:
A bench stands beneath a fairly large tree, next to a small pond. A few
waterlilies float on the surface of the pond and occasionally small waves ripple
across the waters clear surface. A road appears to bend around this area to the
north and east. Long grass grows further west of here and the outline of a
building lies to the southwest.
name: By a Tranquil Pond

desc:
Fairly long grass grows amongst bits of rock slabs, contrasting to the solid
rock surface present north of here. A wooden building borders this grassy area
to the south with a large window facing in this direction. Occasional sounds of
splashing water can be heard from the not too distant east.
name: Beside the Road

desc:
High walls rise up in the north and continue both into the northwest and
northeast. The ground is a mixture of sand and rocks and the only vegetation
here are clusters of green ivy that cling to the solid walls and nearly reach
the top. Sandier regions lie to the west and south while a well-travelled road
is accessible to the east.
name: Beside Massive Walls

desc:
From this vantage point it is impossible to determine where the entrance to the
wooden structure to the south lies. The ground is fairly soft here and seems to
consist mostly of sand and small rocks. A number of boulders and larger rocks
have been piled up into a heap next to the building. The soft sand continues
westwards while more solid ground lies to the north and east.
name: By the Rear of a Building

desc:
The river seems to increase its flow here as it begins to slope even further
into the earth. Directly west a large bridge is visible, but climbing up here
anywhere to reach it would be impossible. The river is simply too fast and
powerful to allow for any manuvering to the side. The only directions in which
travel is possible are eastwards upstream or underneath the bridge in the west.
name: A Fast Flowing River

desc:
This river flows by quite far below the lands to the north and south of it.
Steep slopes rise up in those directions, their crumbling surfaces making
climbing undesirable. Many boulders have fallen into the river forcing it to
flow around them in some areas. These obstructions, combined with the high speed
of the water present a dangerous obstacle course for anything trying to get
through here alive. The current presses on westward leaving little time to
ponder the consequences of false movements.
name: A Dangerous River

desc:
The flow of this river is redirected here by solid rockwalls, forcing the water
to travel westwards. The eastern rivers edge shows signs of erosion from the
constant pressure of the water passing through here. Occasional sprays of water
occur whenever the river flows over semi submerged boulders. The slope leading
up on the northern side appears to present some spots that would allow for a
somewhat safe climb.
name: Bend in a Turbulent River

desc:
A massive lone oak tree grows in the middle of this field surrounded by long
thick grass. A stream borders the field both to the south and west with steep
slopes reaching down to the water. East the field continues along the stream
towards a colourful patch of flowers. Bushy regions of land lie northwards.
name: Among Long Grass

desc:
The river is quite high here, but still far from reaching the top of the
riverbanks on the northern and southern sides. Steep slopes lead up on both
sides, suggesting that at one time much more water was flowing through here.
Nevertheless, the river is still quite substantial and is flowing at a high rate
westwards. It is impossible to make out anything beneath the water surface here.
name: A Fast Flowing River

desc:
The water here is quite turbulent as a small waterfall creates fairly high
waves. The river bends quite abruptly northwards as it encounters a solid
rockwall. Travelling upstream towards the east would be difficult due to the
strong current coming from that direction. Occasionally grey shapes can be seen
swimming passed beneath you.
name: Bend in a River

desc:
The bridge above blocks out any view of the sky. From this vantage point the
bridge is situated quite high up from the river and appears to have been built
very sturdily. The river flows through here at high speed and plummets towards
the north into darkness.
name: Beneath a Large Bridge

desc:
You find yourself within a dug-out hole in a hill. The only exit is through some
long grass covering an opening to the west. Earth and some feathers line the
ground. Strong animal odours along with the cramped space of this hollow make it
quite difficult to breathe in this room.
name: A Fox Hollow

desc:
Rocky hills lie before you, the sharp rocks making your travel hard and slow.
Small windswept bushes seem to be the only thing that grow here, making the
landscape very unappealing to travel through. The hills continue to the west and
north, but to the south and east the steep hills end in a river.
name: Tower Hills

desc:
The terrain here is rough, not hard to travel in but a bit more tiresome than
the green fields to the west. Some small bushes grow here and an occasional tree
provides shade on hot, sunny, summer days. The hills continue to the east and
south, becoming higher and less accessible.
name: Tower Hills

desc:
The hills are quite rough here, quickly draining the energy from your legs. To
the south-east a tall tower can be seen on top of the highest hill. Heading
south will take you to a river that slowly flows through these hills.
name: Tower Hills

desc:
Steep, rocky walls slope down into the river to the east and south. Some small
cracks in the rock make it possible for the skilled climber to make it down to
the river, but the inexperienced traveller maybe should head back to the west
where the hills don't look so impassable.
name: High Cliffs

desc:
Only low, defeated-looking shrubs and coarse grass grow in this rocky terrain. A
large rock lies here, partly buried in the ground. To the east, an unclimable
outcropping rises abruptly; to the south, a wild river runs from east to west.
name: Bottom of a Hill

desc:
Grass grows thick and the ground is very broken. In the summer wild flowers
cover these uneven fields and provide multitudes of small insects a never-ending
feast. In such times flocks of swallows demonstrate their aerial skills. The
tireless hunters eat their fill from the inexhaustible supply of swarming
creatures. There is a long chain of hills to the north which extends from the
west to east. A large bush grows at the foot of the steep hillside.
name: Bushy Grassland

desc:
Between the grassy hills this long vale stretches from the east to west. Golden
grass grows thick all around. Some large stones, half buried in the ground,
stand up from the grass. Steep hillsides form tall walls of the vale to the
north and south. To the west the valley opens out to some bushy grasslands. In
the east is a trail through the hills, crossing the vale.
name: Long Narrow Vale

desc:
You are inside a simple home built on a large tree, the only furniture being a
pair of chairs and a small table. Large windows in the east and west walls give
a view of the surrounding willows and poplars. There are exits to the north and
south, and a trapdoor gives access to the world below.
name: Lethorien's Tree House

desc:
Here the walls are covered with wooden shelves. The cool air smells slightly
moldy. Piles and piles of berry jars are placed on the shelves. This cellar,
deep under the hill, is supported by a couple of large oak beams supporting the
heavy earthen roof. The floor is covered with flat stones, acting as floortiles.
Following them, they lead to an outside door and into another room cut deep into
the hill. A simple wooden door separates the two rooms.
name: Cold Cellar

desc:
Air is very cool here. Sound of dripping water echoes somewhere in the shadows.
Room is quite small. In the back wall three wide shelves are constructed from
wood. Upon the shelves are some cheese moulds and some aging cheese. There is a
distinct aroma of goat milk here. The floor is built from long wooden rods. Some
old skins lie in one corner filled with milk.
name: Cheese Cellar

desc:
This attic probably looks as it always did. Light shines in through narrow slits
in the ceiling, and the water damaged floor seems unstable. You make your way
around the cobwebs and realise that not much is up here.
name: Attic

desc:
You have climbed out onto a branch of the tree. From here you have a fine view
of the river below and to the north, a small stream to the east, and the
grasslands surrounding the tree.
name: Out on a Limb

desc:
You are within a small alcove of the tree house, which appears to be a simple
sleeping area. A pile of soft furs is on the floor, and a few hooks adorn the
southern wall. You can return to the living area through the opening in the
north wall.
name: A Small Alcove

desc:
This tunnel must have one time been completely filled with water. Though there
is still a large amount of water passing through here now, it does not reach the
top of the passage, thus allowing for some breathing room. The entire passage
consists of smooth rock, undoubtedly molded by water. Rushing water leads west
towards what seems to be the outside, however there appears to be a steep drop
in that direction.
name: Within a Rocky Passage

desc:
A small stream flowing eastward flows under an old wooden bridge here. Although
there are a few mossy corners where perpetual shade reigns, the space under the
bridge is breezy and well lit when the sun or moon is in the sky. The stream,
which is easily waded, flows gently over old grey rounded rocks and makes a
musical sound as it trickles toward the sea.
name: Underneath a Bridge

desc:
This is the mouth of a small forest stream which joins the great Lhun here. It
is shallow enough to wade, but the banks are steep. Westwards one can see the
outline of a bridge. The trees grow closer together to the east, casting a dark
shadow on the ground below them.
name: Trickling Stream

desc:
The little hairs on the back of your neck curl as your look to the east and see
that the thicket becomes dark and foreboding. A nagging feeling of being watched
doesn't help you to feel any safer, yet curiousity provokes you to travel on
further.
name: Thicket

desc:
The darkness and eerie quietness of this area suddenly both calms your fears and
tightens the grip on your weapon. You no longer hear the carefree chirping of
birds, and you sense a lurking danger nearby.
name: Dense Thicket

desc:
You stop suddenly as you look around you and wonder in amazement at how you got
so far into such an unpleasant area. Your body has grown weary of the branches
and small bushes in your way. This might be a good place to rest before going
further.
name: Thicket

desc:
There is little space in this room but the wooden walls and solid roof shelter
this place from the elements. The southern section is blocked off by a thick
log, which forms some sort counter. The entire room before, behind, and on that
counter is cluttered with goods. Baskets stand on crates along the walls, while
sacks are stapled or hanged wherever they fit. The western wall is covered with
massive boards which again bear countless items of day-to-day use.
name: A Shelter Cluttered with Goods

desc:
You are near the centre of a narrow island in the River Lhun, whose long
dimension is north and south, parallel to the river's flow. To the east and west
the water laps at the island's muddy shore. Tall oaks and great old gnarled
maples rise up here from the lush undergrowth.
name: Island in the River Lhun

desc:
This is the southernmost point of a long north-south island in the River Lhun
which separates two of its channels. To the north tall broad-leafed trees rise
up from the fertile, oft-flooded soil. To the south a stiffer breeze ruffles the
broad surface of the Lhun, which looks more like a great lake or estuary than a
mere stream.
name: Southern End of an Island

desc:
The river's water swirls slowly but powerfully here, as the current from a side
channel to the west merges with the vast main southward flow. To the southwest
spume rises from a rocky point; you can barely see the eastward bank of the
river, which has more the look of a lake or strait here. North of here you see
some trees on a point of land.
name: River Lhun

desc:
You are floating on what seems to be the calmer part of the river. East of here
is another part of the river that seems to border a trail. You can also continue
south along the river.
name: River Lhun

desc:
The river is here floating from north to south at a pleasant speed. As you look
east, you see a trail following the river. But west of here is a stronger part
of the river. These waters seem quite safe, but not as safe as having the firm
ground under your feet.
name: River Lhun

desc:
The river is floating from north to south at a pleasant speed. You can see that
the current to the west is somewhat stronger, but you sense that it is quite
safe.
name: River Lhun

desc:
The river continues calmly along here, and you begin to relax your tightened
muscles during your long and tabescent journey. To the distant east, you can
make out a well-used path with some bushes behind it. You can continue along the
river in all other directions.
name: River Lhun

desc:
The river is here floating from north to south at pleasant speed. The strong
part of the river is west of here. A small stream comes from the east here. You
are able to enter the trail from here.
name: River Lhun

desc:
This part of the river is calmer than the surrounding areas since the main
currents flows closely to the western shore. This section is quite shallow,
nowhere deeper than five feet, and filled with under-water vegetation and such.
Here and there sandbanks can be discerned in the muddy water, no doubt a peril
for the fishing-boats and such vessels that traffic the river.
name: The Great River Lhun

desc:
Slowly, almost lazily, the great river flows southwards here. Looking west one
realizes that the river speeds southwards more speedily there. The water here is
coloured by the mud that the river brings with it from the north but also tanged
with the rich blue sea-water. There is a smell of seaweeds and salt mixed with
musty odors from the surrounding woods here.
name: The Gulf of Lhun

desc:
Here the great river Lhun reaches its end and flows out into a great gulf. The
water is yellow and opaque because of the mud and the debris that the river has
brought with it all the way from the distant Blue Mountains. To enter the shore
to the east seems to be impossible due to the twisted weeds and rotting logs
that litter the shore. It is possible to travel north on the river or south onto
the gulf.
name: The Gulf of Lhun

desc:
A salty breeze blows in steadily from the sea filling the air with the special
smell that is prevalent in this area. Although the Lhun lies some distance to
the north the water is muddy here. South and west the gulf opens up into what in
fact is a good-sized sea while to the east a swampy shore blocks all obvious
passage.
name: The Gulf of Lhun

desc:
One can guess that the water is quite deep here, dark as it is. Looking in all
directions save to the east one can see the far-stretching surface of the gulf
of Lhun. To the east it is possible to land onto something that looks like a dry
patch of land. Further to the north lies the vast river Lhun which slowly flows
through the lands.
name: The Gulf of Lhun

desc:
The black waters of the gulf stretch out in all directions from here. The motion
of the waves is reduced however, as the water becomes shallower both here and
further east. In the north and south travel can continue upon water along the
coast. A small haven of calm water lies to the east.
name: The Gulf of Lhun

desc:
The action of the water keeps threatening to push you onto sharp rocks to the
east. High cliffs rise in that direction and it would be impossible to leave the
turbulent waters here. Choosing a direction of travel is nearly impossible since
you are continually being thrown about by strong waves. The coastal waters
continue to the north and south while the open sea stretches westwards. A
continuous stream of water gushes out of a hole halfway up the eastern rockwall.
name: Amidst Rough Waters

desc:
Rock walls rise high up in the east and south. Their surfaces are smooth and
glisten with water droplets making climbing them impossible. The water is clear
and white caps are evidence of numerous waves breaking around you. Below you,
despite the clear water, a cold darkness prevents any clues as to the depth of
the water. Open water lies westwards.
name: Amidst Rough Waters

desc:
The river grows wider here as it mixes its waters with the eastern tributary. To
the east you can see a delta with an impressive bridge leaping over it. The
current is somewhat stronger here. You can see a large island covered with trees
to the west.
name: River Lhun

desc:
The river flows lazily from the north to the south. The water is very deep, but
you can see something glinting deep down. To the west, you see a a large island,
inviting you to take a rest.
name: River Lhun

desc:
The river grows real wide here, flowing slowly through the flat landscape. It
continues west and south. East of here, you are able to spot a trail.
name: River Lhun

desc:
The river floats steadily along here, calming your fears about what might exist
so far from the towns. You can continue onto the river going west, or you could
go to the trail south. The river continues to the north too, enraged.
name: River Lhun

desc:
A rockwall rises up directly south of here while low land can be found both to
the east and north. These land masses have reduced the strength of the waves
allowing the waters to calm down. Some seaweed circles among the waves in clear
waters. The ground below is slightly sandy but mostly solid rock. The eastern
shore can be reached easily while open and more turbulent waters lie westward.
name: Sheltered Bay

desc:
Beyond a wild orchard and a wild garden a fairly large building stands to the
west and south of here, no windows facing towards this side. Beds of flowers and
herbs lie close to the building, their blooms attracting a variety of insects. A
tiny path continues south along the mostly untamed vegetation and fades towards
the north.
name: A Path between an Orchard and a Wet Meadow

desc:
This meadow is swampy: water-liking vegetation of all kinds has been growing
nearly without restriction. The ground is more murky and muddy than actual
water: old, decaying vegetation can be seen and smelled everywhere.
name: A Swampy, Wild Meadow

desc:
This orchard is either not attended to on purpose or has grown wild due to
neglect. The trees are mostly old, the grass on the ground grows high and is
only broken by occasional spots of larger bushes. Some of the trees are so old
and gnarly that they are barely recogniseable as trees and could very well be
old bushes.
name: Wild Orchard of Apple-trees

desc:
This was once a great elven building, but it has fallen in disuse years ago.
Time, rain, and the winds have taken their toll as the roof is collapsed, the
decorations on the walls are fading and the walls shelter all kinds of thriving
bushes, plants, and flowers from harsh weather.
name: An Apple-Tree within an Old Ruin

desc:
A dry and warm cavern, its earthen walls and floor scattered with plenty of
nooks and smaller holes. Higher up on the walls, patches of muddy granite shows
through the hardened earth. Some roots and twigs cover the eastern flank of this
place.
name: Snake Lair

desc:
A large growth of old fungus grows thick on the walls and causes this small cave
to reek like an old dwarven war boot just after a campaign. The shape of the
walls suggests that this cave was not created just by the forces of nature. The
ceiling seems to have been made into a dome. East is a broken doorway to another
cavern. The western wall is covered by a large patch of mold. The cave continues
south.
name: Moist Cave

desc:
The broken stone shelves on the floor and scattered on the walls suggest that
this was a store room for some ancient dwellers. The floor is muddy making it
slippery. Two arches rise from the corners of the room and cross in the middle
of the ceiling, shaped into somewhat of a dome. Some airducts are worked into
the ceiling but no light filters through them. A doorway leading to the west is
partially collapsed and if it ever held a door, no remains of it are left.
name: Old Storage Room

desc:
A crack runs through the low roof of this slighty damp cave. The smooth floor
and walls suggest that this cave was carved by rushing water long ago. It is now
large enough to allow an easy passage. There is a faint draft of cool air coming
from the vegetation southwards.
name: Inside a Small Cave

desc:
The tunnel is dug through the stone. The walls are supported here and there by
roughly hewn stones. The ceiling is cut into an arch. There are doorways to the
north and east. In the south the tunnel has collapsed providing no passage.
Water drips down from the ceiling. A large puddle is formed in the middle of the
room.
name: Connecting Tunnel

desc:
This room is perfectly round and its roof domed. The chamber is surprisingly
well preserved from harm when compared to other rooms in the cave. A stone grate
is constructed in the floor allowing the water to drain and in the walls, near
the curve of the ceiling, are some ventilation ducts which keep the air here
quite dry. However, this has not prevented the decay of the arms which must once
have been here, for there are none; only a weapon rack made from polished stone
stands against one wall.
name: Old Weapon Storage

desc:
Steep slopes descend on every side from this low hilltop except to the east,
where the hillside rises at a very sharp angle, forming an impassable cliff.
There is more soil here than on many of the hills, allowing a profusion of
wildflowers to bloom: purplish-pink catchfly, orange hawkweed, blue milkwort,
and dozens of other species in a myriad of colours.
name: Flowery Hilltop

desc:
Rocky slopes rise on every side of this valley, nestled deep into the Tower
Hills. Loose stones litter the hillsides and leave little room for grass and
weeds, but a great deal of moss grows in the shadows of larger boulders. There
are several dry gullies leading into the valley, but except during the wettest
seasons there is no sign of water.
name: Deep Valley

desc:
Steep slopes rise and fall on every side, created by folds in the underlying
stone. Thin soil clings to the slopes, providing just enough nourishment for
thin, yellowish grass and a few stunted bushes. Pale grey and orange boulders
thrust through the soil. Just to the south, the hills descend into a deep
valley.
name: Tower Hills

desc:
The Tower Hills, called Emyn Beraid in the tongue of the elves, rise and fall in
every direction. The hills are rocky and steep-sided, with extensive areas of
exposed rock and little clinging vegetation. A few scrapes on the rocks and
abandoned nests left in nooks and cracks attest to the periodic presence of
wildlife here.
name: Tower Hills

desc:
This nook is located below high ridgelines and cliffs in all directions except
the south. Being such a protected area from the wind and elements has allowed
heavy brush, grasses, and even the occasional tree to flurish here. Along with
the growth here, signs of animals can be seen in the passages through the brush
and grass.
name: Hilly Nook

desc:
The terrain here is hilly, and there is a gradual upward slope to the south. Far
to the south-east, a high ridge dominates the sky-line. A few grasses and shrubs
have managed to take root in the rocky soil, whilst in other areas the soil has
been swept away by weather and wind, and the bedrock lies bare.
name: Rising Hills

desc:
A rocky ledge has formed here as hills from the north rise up and then flatten
out as they run to the east. A sharp ledge sits to the south and an enclosed
area can be seen down below. Eastward, the hills and ridgelines continue to
climb toward a high peak.
name: Rocky Ledge

desc:
The hills that form this area rises to the south-east. To the north, the hills
run together to form a small ridgeline that descends from the higher ground. As
the hills continue to climb to the south-east they approach the crown of the
large hill. Some grass covers the ground and the occasional lonely bush can be
seen as well.
name: Sloping Hills

desc:
The hills narrow to form a ridgeline that connects the lower hills to the north,
to the high area to the south. The terrain to the south-east of here continues
to rise to a high peak that can be seen in the distance. The ridgeline is
covered by rocks and boulders, making it difficult, but still possible to
navigate. While it is not very wide, it is safe as long as one continues to
follow it. There is no way to descend from here to the east or west.
name: Ascending Ridgeline

desc:
This area has hills all around. There is no obvious path to travel here, but the
surroundings do not appear to be difficult to navigate. A slightly taller hill
can be seen to the east above the rest of the hills that dot the horizon.
name: Rolling Hills

desc:
The area here slopes gently upward toward a hill to the east that appears to be
slightly taller than the surrounding ones. Broken rocks can be seen scattered
across the hillside but there is enough room in-between them to ascend it
without too much trouble.
name: Sloping Hillside

desc:
These hills show all the signs of being beaten down year after year by the
unforgiving wind. The undulating hills continue in all directions, and it
appears that they would be relatively easy to travel around or traverse.
name: Low Rolling Hills

desc:
This crevice between the hills is hidden from sight until it is about ten feet
away. It seems to have been formed by a long dry underground spring, and it is
very shady here. The crevice slants down further underground to the east, where
the faint rustling sounds and odour of guano can be detected.
name: Hidden Crevice

desc:
Small rocks are scattered around a well-tended trail that leads uphill to the
east, and descends into a valley to the south. The path takes a detour around a
very large boulder that is completely covered with moss and seems quite
impossible to climb on top of. A small stream that seems to pop out of the
ground is running in a northerly direction. On top of the hill to the east
stands a high, white tower.
name: Trail by a Large Boulder

desc:
This valley cuts very deep through the Tower Hills, so deep that the sun rarely
reaches its bottom. Because of that there is almost no vegetation here, only a
few small tussocks of grass. A well-tended path runs through the valley in
north-south direction while to the east and west the hillsides are so steep that
they seem impossible to climb, even for an experienced climber.
name: Trail through a Valley in the Tower Hills

desc:
Here the hills completely surround the wide, paved path that runs through the
valley, bending gently along the valley floor towards the south, and breaking
through a small opening to the north. The vegetation is very sparse, probably
because the sun almost never reaches the bottom of this deep valley. The sides
of the valley are so steep that it seems impossible to climb them at all.
name: Trail through a Valley in the Tower Hills

desc:
A shallow-looking ford has formed here where a well-tended path crosses a rocky
brook. The brook flows between the cliffs, its water flowing in lazily from the
east, crossing the shallow ford, then gathering speed as the brook narrows
towards the west. An occasional fish can be seen, leaping high above the water
surface. A stone-paved path runs from the ford, disappearing between the cliffs
to the north, and connecting to a larger road to the south.
name: Ford over a Rocky Brook

desc:
Tall hills rise to the east and southwest of the road, which winds its way
through the levelest path it can find. The road is worn and well-travelled, but
still provides the easiest means of moving through this hilly area. The road
runs nearly straight to the west, while it passes through several bends and
twists before finally extending to the south.
name: Road to the Grey Havens

desc:
The Tower Hills rise on every side of this valley except to the south, where the
valley continues. Enormous boulders of grey, brown, and pale orange lie
scattered across the valley floor, accompanied by millions of smaller stones.
There is little room left for vegetation, but grass and weeds have taken root in
every available crack and crevice.
name: Rocky Valley

desc:
The boulders that cover these hills are very large and worn down by the tides of
time. Among the boulders, small green bushes grow. Elsewhere the ground is
covered by soft, green grass that almost reaches a man's waist. A road crosses
the hills to the south and a small path leaves it, leading north at the foot of
the western slope.
name: Hills by the Road

desc:
Many large rocks are scattered across the hillsides. Some of these rocks are so
large that whole houses could be built on top of them. Far to the west you can
distinguish a small mountain path winding its way through the hills, but to the
north and south the hills continue uninterruptedly. The hills grow steeper
towards the east, as the Tower Hills rises ever higher.
name: Tower Hills

desc:
A steep hillside completely blocks the way to the north here. Soft, green, grass
covers the ground like a thick carpet. Half-concealed by the grass, many large
round stones are scattered all across the hillsides. To the south, east, and
west the mighty Tower Hills continue.
name: Tower Hills

desc:
Small green bushes grow here and there between the patches of grass, which have
been chewed almost down to their roots. This is the highest point on the hill;
all around you see similar formations, like waves in the ocean. The north side
of the hill is broken and steep. To the south the ground slopes down, just to
rise up again into another hill. A large rock stands on the hill summit; on top
of it a small birch has taken root. Beneath the rocks are some remains of an
animal, decaying among the shrubs.
name: Grazed Hills

desc:
The hillside slopes down toward a tall, rocky ridge, which looms high into the
sky, blocking the way to the south. However, the hill itself slopes quite
gently, and is covered by thick, deep green grass, and a scattering of small
shrubs and bushes.
name: Below the Ridge

desc:
The top of the hill lays to the south and it slopes northward. A small grassy
patch lies between the shoulders of the hill. In the west the hill slopes down
to some flatter ground. Rock and grass is all that's here.
name: Sloping Hillside

desc:
All around the hills grow taller. Here however the wind that blows hard upon the
hills is blocked by them. Small gooseberry bushes grow here sheltered from the
wind.
name: Depression between Two Hills

desc:
Shrubs and stones cover the hills all around. This one is no different from the
others. Traversing these hills and the dust from the dry grass is making you
dizzy. To the south and west, the hill slopes downward.
name: Hills

desc:
Small thorny bushes grow wild on the ground. In the west the hill slopes down to
a valley. In all other directions the hills grow taller.
name: Western Slope

desc:
A tall ridge extending from the west to east prevents passage to the south. In
the west the hillside drops suddenly down to the valley below. In the north
seems to be a way down. In the east the hills continue. Ground is covered by
grasses and shrubs. Some animals seem to be moving above the ridge, but
unfortunately the grasses and bushes that grow upon it hide them from sight.
name: Between Ridge and Cliff

desc:
Some gentle hills rise above a pleasant glen between the hills to the south.
Grass has been eaten very short. The small bushes here and there have also lost
some of their cover. Little plants with yellow flowers dot the ground, obviously
shunned by the animals that graze here. To the north the hills come to an abrupt
end and fall down many feet before reaching the level ground. To the west is a
small forest. To the east the hills continue.
name: Grazed Hills

desc:
Small green bushes grow here and there among the grass that covers the hills. A
narrow wagon trail weaves a path between hills, keeping to the low ground to
avoid steep slopes and other obstacles. A steep cliff to the west prevents
access in that direction, and at the bottom of the cliff, nestled amidst
vegetation and rocks, a small shingled roof peeks out.
name: Dusty Wagon Trail

desc:
A dust-covered wagon trail weaves a path through shrub-covered hills and between
boulders. A few large, thorny bushes grow on the west-facing slopes, but the
area is devoid of trees. The hills grow steeper and taller towards the east,
showing what inspired the name Tower Hills. Yet even so, the trail finds a route
that avoids the worst of slopes and other obstacles.
name: Dusty Wagon Trail

desc:
A wagon trail runs through the area here heading west and east. The trail shows
signs of being fairly old, but is still usable. The rocky ground that is under
the trail shows signs of breaking through at some points and is working on
holding back the weeds that are attempting to gain a foothold. Hills slope
upwards from the trail to the north and south.
name: Wagon Trail

desc:
Called Emyn Beraid by the elves, the Tower Hills are ancient. Despite their age,
time and erosion have not been able to completely blunt their edges, and sharp,
narrow ridges thrust up between steep slopes. A thin layer of soil clings to the
lower slopes, supporting coarse grass and weeds, along with a few stunted shrubs
that bear pale yellow blossoms.
name: Tower Hills

desc:
Large boulders are scattered across the landscape. Between the boulders, small
trees and lush green bushes try to survive in the infertile soil. To the west
the hills fall steeply down to a small trail, to the south and the east the
rocky landscape continues. A steep cliff wall bars passage to the north.
name: Tower Hills

desc:
The rock wall to the north, the very wall this ledge sits on, is home to a
number of birds, judging from the bird nests and droppings. Most of them seem to
be empty, but not all of them are. To the west is an extremely steep slope,
covered in loose shale and gravel-sized rock, making the descent difficult but
possible, however coming back up would not be smart to attempt. The ledge
continues to the east, though not for long as it ends at a small opening in the
hillside.
name: High, Flat ledge

desc:
The road runs from the east to the west, surrounded by the mighty Tower Hills.
The surface of the road is smooth, worn by the weather and the passage of many
travellers. A few bushes grow beside the road, providing shelter for small
animals and birds.
name: Road to the Grey Havens

desc:
Surrounded by the mighty Tower Hills, the road runs from the east to the west.
Some small, dense bushes grow beside the road, providing shelter for small
animals and birds. A small trail leads north from here.
name: Road to the Grey Havens

desc:
Here the road bends as one of the large Tower Hills prevents it from running due
west. Instead the road turns south through the magnificent landscape. Small
tufts of grass are spread along the road and many bushes grow on the hillsides
to the north.
name: Road to the Grey Havens

desc:
The road continues to the west and south of here. To the north and east water
can be seen glimmering through the dense bushes that grow along the road. In the
far north-east a large tower is pointing towards the sky.
name: Road to the Grey Havens

desc:
This area is obviously well travelled as indicated by the worn ground. Though
the road consists mostly of earth and little rock it has been pressed solid by
many years of travel and provides solid footing. Dug up earth further south
suggest the presence of a cultivated field. Directly north a river flows towards
the west but its steep muddy slope makes entering it from here impossible.
name: Road to the Grey Havens

desc:
This road continues east and west. A steep slope leads down on the northern side
of the road into a slow moving river. Small patches of grass grow amongst the
pressed down earth that makes up the road. A ploughed field borders the road to
the south and seems to continue into the distance.
name: Road to the Grey Havens

desc:
The Road to Grey Havens bends here around a structure in the west. A sturdy
fence blocks travel in that direction. The main road continues both to the north
and east while a smaller less travelled road branches off leading south. Sounds
of running water reach you from somewhere in the north. The road itself consists
simply of earth that has been pressed flat through years of use.
name: Bend in the Road to the Grey Havens

desc:
A slow moving river prevents travel by foot eastwards with its slope appearing
steep and muddy, making it an unsafe place to enter the water. This road
continues westwards where it will eventually reach the Grey Havens. It also
bends to the south leading to a distant destination.
name: Bend in the Road to the Grey Havens

desc:
A fast moving river borders the road to the north while south a gate allows
entry to an enclosed pasture. The road is fairly straight here leading west and
eastward with little change. Small piles of earth have been dug out of the
ground most likely the remnants of a galloping horse. There are other piles as
well on this road, brown steaming piles that are best not stepped into.
name: Road to the Grey Havens

desc:
Directly south of here stands a wooden building that would likely provide for a
comfortable resting location. Fast-moving water lies northwards, with steep
muddy slopes leading down towards a stream. A well-travelled road continues east
and west adjusting its straight path only occasionally to avoid large trees.
name: Road to the Grey Havens

desc:
Instead of continuing in a straight fashion westwards, the road bends here and
follows along a fast moving river to the north. Long grasses grow beside the
road both to the south and west. Few wild animals spend any length of time on
this road, they are likely trying to avoid the frequent travellers who pass by
here.
name: Bend in the Road to the Grey Havens

desc:
A well travelled road reaches both into the western and southern distance. The
ground is slightly rocky here, though it still consists mostly of pressed down
dirt. Steep rocky slopes lead down to a fast moving body of water both to the
north and east.
name: Bend in a Road

desc:
The ground is nearly solid rock here and drops off quite steeply to the north.
Along the side of the road a few grasses have managed to find a hold amidst the
rocky surface. Further south of here the solid rock gives way to more fertile
ground. The road continues unobstructed towards both the east and west.
name: Road to the Grey Havens

desc:
The road bends abruptly here and passes north over a sturdy bridge. The surface
of this road is virtually solid rock. Vegetation is only capable of growing in
small earth pockets that have developed wherever there are small hollows in the
rock surface. South of here the rock has broken apart somewhat, allowing a
variety of grasses to grow. A sandier region lies westwards.
name: Bend in the Road to the Grey Havens

desc:
Long, sloping hills from the west continue into smaller, more broken terrain as
they march to the east through this land. The ground has spots of broken scree
covering the dips between hills as eons of time have started to break them down
and erode their crowns. The landscape here is fairly barren with only minor
tufts of grass to be seen in the more sheltered areas.
name: Sloping Hills

desc:
Dried pine needles cover the ground, undoubtedly stemming from a large pine tree
outgrowing all other trees in this area. The sounds of water reach you from the
north and southwest. Though there does not exist a path leading through here,
someone felt the need to build a narrow bridge by the western rivers edge.
Further trees grow south of here.
name: A Light Forest

desc:
At first glance, this small bridge appears quite sturdy, but its pine boards
creak and wobble as soon as anyone sets foot upon them, threatening to tip the
unwary into the small stream, which flows lazily from east to west, below. To
the north and south, the bridge is met by an old trail, much worn by the passage
of many feet, over the years.
name: Flimsy Bridge

desc:
Grass and shrubs grow wild all around. The ground is broken, making travel
difficult. Here and there among the grass a large stone sticks out like a giant
head. Some thickets of thorny bushes create an impenetrable wall to the west. A
trail circles this place to the east and to the north.
name: Rough Grasslands

desc:
A small rustic bed is the only piece of furniture in this tiny hut. A few simple
tools hang from pegs, which have been driven into the wall in a neat row, and
above them a plain wooden shelf bears some wooden plates and small boxes. Wind
blows through tiny chinks in the walls, and a square opening, serving as a
window, is unglazed. Clearly this must be the home of one who has little care
for treasures or comfort.
name: Goatherd's Hut

desc:
The boulders to the south and the brush to the north make life miserable here.
In some places bushes grow so close to the boulders that the passage must be won
by climbing the boulders themselves. In others, the boulders have rolled across
the path and then an exhausting attempt at penetrating the brush must be made.
Both the brush and the boulders look unappealing. No sound of birds can be
heard, and even insects are still.
name: Between Brush and Boulder

desc:
The shores narrows somewhat here, making the current strong and powerful. You
have no other choice but to follow the tributary west, or struggle your way
upstreams to the east.
name: Tributary of the River

desc:
The shores narrows somewhat here, making the current strong and powerful. You
have no other choice but to follow the tributary west, or struggle your way
upstreams to the east.
name: Tributary of the River
